posts.name: alan weinberg
posts.cooked:
if you could have a “do over” and take a vinous Mulligan, what would you do beside building the cellar twice as big?

For me, I would have bought a lot more “lesser” red Burgs in top vintages–AC and premier crus that would have allowed more quantity than the same dollars for grand crus.

posts.name: Michael S. Monie
posts.cooked: That red Burgundy plays such a minor role in my enjoyment of wine.

posts.name: p a u l b a t t a g l i a
posts.cooked: Instead of 50% champagne, my cellar would be 100% champagne.
posts.display_username: p a u l b a t t a g l i a

posts.name: Berry Crawford
posts.cooked: I would have filled a cellar full of Beaujolais and Jura reds. I really regret selling my 1996 Salon. I wish I had known about truchot a few years earlier.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I wish I had bought more Truchot.

posts.name: John Morris
posts.cooked:
I regret the lapse in my purchases of Northern Rhones between the late 1980s and mid-2000s, and I wish I had become interested in Barolo before 1996 and could have bought more pre-1990 wines.

Happily, I have very few regrets in what I did buy. I don’t really have anything I’d like to dump.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

For me, I would have bought a lot more “lesser” red Burgs in top vintages–AC and premier crus that would have allowed more quantity than the same dollars for grand crus.

Well, aside from the obvious “wish I had seen the price escalation coming and bought more of the impossible to afford wines”, I actually wish I had done just the opposite of your regret - buy fewer lower end bottlings, and spend the same money (or more, see preface) on fewer higher end bottles. You can almost always find good, relatively affordable daily drinkers, but the real cherries are much harder to afford and acquire today.

posts.name: Henry Kiichli
posts.cooked:
I went to a tasting in the early 90s of the 1990 Bx. They were all stellar - each one better than the last. I bought a few, and still have Leo Bart and Troplong M. The prices were in the 30s D Marks.

I should have bought all the TM I could. Mostly for me, but the resale value went through the roof.

posts.name: Peter Kleban
posts.cooked: My biggest regret is not getting more interested in wine years ago. We get too soon old, and too late smart! (I know, what else is new)

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Peter Kleban:

We get too soon old, and too late smart!

Wise words that could be used in almost any discussion

posts.name: Loren Sonkin
posts.cooked:
Not drunk my Bordeaux and best Cal Cabs so young.
Bought some of the crazy priced wine, just to have them now (DRC, First growths, La-Las, Grange).
Kept track of who was at the many offlines I have been to not just what wines we drank.

posts.name: Keith Levenberg
posts.cooked: Another vote for ‘should have bought more Truchot,’ plus ‘should have bought more Clos Roche Blanche.’

posts.name: Eric Ifune
posts.cooked: That mixed 1990 DRC case offered for $4500 keeps me awake at night.

posts.name: Joe B
posts.cooked: I wish i had bought fifty cases of tge 2012 caymus at $60 per. Today they are on winebid at $100 and i have seen them go higher. Sell my fifty cases for tidy profit and buy some Realm.

posts.name: Mike DiSalvo
posts.cooked: Would have bought more wines (at lower prices) from out of state retailers and would have purchased more french wines. Also, would not have opened some of the California wines so early.

I’d like the dollars I spent on great deals for very good wine back to convert to very good deals for great wine. I have too many cases of very nice wine that, given excessive overall volume, I should have swapped for a few bottles of great wine.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Wish I would have bought more Cabs from '95 and '01, and less '97 and '99. Actually, no '99 and only select bottles of '97 and saved that money to put towards more '01.

Wish I had a way-back machine so I could buy select Burgundies from '83, '85, '90, and '93 before they went ballistic in price.

Wish I knew about Riesling a couple decades ago.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked:
Joe B:

I wish i had bought fifty cases of tge 2012 caymus at $60 per.

Got 6 bottles from Total Wine for $55 each before the craze started. I don’t even like it.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Not trying to tell people what they should say, but a thread like this would be a lot more interesting if it weren’t “I wish I had bought a lot of [great wine that’s super expensive now] back when it was cheap.” That seems to go without saying for everyone.

I personally find that I really like aged Riesling, and it doesn’t even have to be expensive or highly rated riesling. The wine character really emerges at 10-20 years old, even for just solid $20 type riesling, and it’s so much more interesting than the sweet green grapes flavor they all have when they’re young. But it’s hard to find aged ones at retail. I wish I had been setting aside a case a year from all but the lousy vintages, so I’d have nice aged riesling to pull from my cellar. I should start doing it now, really.

posts.name: Harry Gruber
posts.cooked: I drank far too many wines when they were too young, e.g., 1978 Burgs, and 1971 & 1975 German Rieslings.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Chris Seiber:

I wish I had been setting aside a case a year from all but the lousy vintages, so I’d have nice aged riesling to pull from my cellar. I should start doing it now, really.

I wish you had posted this a couple of years ago, when I started thinning out my over-stocked Riesling cellar

posts.name: Kevin Porter
posts.cooked:
“Too few to mention…” after all, it’s the journey, right?

That said, I do wish I had a cellar full of nebbiolo, burgundy and riesling rather than CA Pinot and CDP.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Funny, Kevin. Though it’s hard for me to really think of regrets-- other than not buying more Burgundy to age early on (when I felt it only took 5-10 years for RB to become “mature”, instead of 20 or so), I have more “glad I did that”. And, one of the things I am glad I did was, by 1990 figuring out I really didn’t like CA wines at all, and that CDP, which I bought a lot of in the '80s, was too alcoholic for my tastes (and the vintages were becoming increasingly so by 1989-1990).

And…truthfully…I don’t have any major regrets…or even things I’d have done differently. I figured out a “plan” early…and am pleased with the results almost totally. Never really thought much about that.

And, I don’t even regret not buying more Truchot.

Maybe my biggest “wine regret” is not being able to turn our sons (28 and 21) onto wine much at all. They both think of the many wine visits to Burgundy and Alsace as child abuse…and, I think, that made them a little close minded. (The older one managed to ruin a few visits, though…by running and climbing around and disracting everyone; he really annoyed Henri Jayer in 1992 by climbing on onto the barrel pyramids.)

posts.name: Ken Vastola
posts.cooked: I should have bought more Giacosa from the 82, 86, 89 vintages while they were still cheap.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked: Ironically, Ken…Giacosa seemed very expensive to me “back in the day” (an expression I hate) when they “were still cheap”.

posts.name: Adam Kuehn
posts.cooked: I wish I’d have constructed a “real” cellar instead of buying a wine fridge (and then another one). It would be so much nicer having a dedicated wine space. But it was too expensive then and it’s way too expensive now, so I’ll have to leave that dream unfulfilled for the foreseeable future.

posts.name: Chuck Miller
posts.cooked: I wish I had bought every bottle of 1990 Rayas @$70 per back in 1992, instead of the 6 I did buy. The only downside to having 10-20 cases of that is that with repeated tastings, it might lose some of the things that make it special.

posts.name: Fred C
posts.cooked:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Maybe my biggest “wine regret” is not being able to turn our sons (28 and 21) onto wine much at all. They both think of the many wine visits to Burgundy and Alsace as child abuse…and, I think, that made them a little close minded.

Perhaps they resented you harping on them for being too young?

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked: Not buying case after case of 2008 First Growths at 100EUR per

posts.name: Bob Hunnicutt
posts.cooked: Buying too much Cab years ago just like I’m buying too much Pinot now

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
In the big picture, my wine regrets are only slightly bigger than choosing the pastrami instead of the Reuben at lunch, but…

Not learning enough about Burgundy early on, to have confidence to buy it.

No Nebbiolo.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Fred C:
Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Maybe my biggest “wine regret” is not being able to turn our sons (28 and 21) onto wine much at all. They both think of the many wine visits to Burgundy and Alsace as child abuse…and, I think, that made them a little close minded.

Perhaps they resented you harping on them for being too young?

I think it was more likely the leashes.

Charles Rousseau liked one of the leashes so much he offered to trade a bottle for it. I told him “none of that old wine; I wasn’t born yesterday. Only new wine for me.” https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7sELqobCIXU

posts.name: Brian King
posts.cooked: I wish I had my current income 15-20 years ago, so when I was passing on Rousseau Chambertin for $200 and Clos St Jacques for $80 because they were too expensive, I could actually be buying

When I first got into wine 5ish years ago, I found wine boards and bought what I thought I should like based on the threads and discussion.

I now realize that I really like CA wines and don’t have much of a desire to stray, aside from a a very few categories.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: Declining my former partner’s offer to share some LaTache back in the early 1990s. Not sure of the vintage, but does it matter?

posts.cooked:
AKuehn:

I wish I’d have constructed a “real” cellar instead of buying a wine fridge (and then another one). It would be so much nicer having a dedicated wine space. But it was too expensive then and it’s way too expensive now, so I’ll have to leave that dream unfulfilled for the foreseeable future.

Waiting so long to do it. I finally bit the bullet a couple years ago and WOW has it made everything so much more fun.

posts.name: Jay Miller
posts.cooked:
Keith Levenberg:

… plus ‘should have bought more Clos Roche Blanche.’

I’ll change that slightly to “should have cellared more Clos Roche Blanche.” I kept buying it but I kept drinking it.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Regrets, I’ve drank a few
But then again, too few to mention
I sipped what I had to sip and saw it through without exemption
I didn’t plan each vintage course, no careful trips along the Napa Highway
And more, much more than this, I did it my way

Yes, there were times, that’s what I think
When I uncorked more than my palate could drink
But through it all, when there was doubt
I sucked it up then spit it out
I faced it all and I stood wobbly but tall and drank it my way.

posts.name: Dennis Kanagie
posts.cooked: Nice one Frank Winatra.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
John Morris:

I regret the lapse in my purchases of Northern Rhones between the late 1980s and mid-2000s.

This would be one of my top regrets. Loved Northern Rhone when first introduced to it in 1994, wish I had bought everything I could. Sadly, availability in Florida was terrible and we had prohibitions against shipping wine into the state. I’ve been buying since 2005 but not enough, need to backfill.

Wish I had skipped 2005 St Ems and 2007 Chateauneuf du Papes. I recently traded my remaining stash of '05 St Ems and am close to clearing out my remaining '07 CDPs. Would have loved to have used that money for '05 left banks.

Wish I had not consumed most of the Bordeaux I bought in the 1990s way too early, but in my defense, they would have all died anyway in 2004 when we had 3 back-to-back hurricanes in Orlando and I lost power for many days on end. In the height of summer. The wine fridge had no power, it died too.

posts.name: Rainer Guntermann
posts.cooked: to have bought too many Rhone wines from 1998 - 2007

posts.name: Jeremy Cuthbertson
posts.cooked: Easy. Signing up too many lists during our first few trips to CA wine country.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

in my defense, they would have all died anyway in 2004 when we had 3 back-to-back hurricanes in Orlando and I lost power for many days on end.

And yet your biggest regret is not living in Florida

In the early years of collecting ( '87-'95 ) , I should’ve bought less diversity and focused more on the wines I loved in great years. 82/83/85/86 Ramonets/leflaives/Coche,90/91 DRC’s . Older Bordeauxs 59/61, more Rayas and Chave and definitely more Reislings and Champagnes
I would have bought all 11 bottled of 1996 Sir Winston Churchill off wined.com for $150 back in 2009.

For me, I would have bought a lot more “lesser” red Burgs in top vintages–AC and premier crus that would have allowed more quantity than the same dollars for grand crus.

posts.display_username: W E R D N A - S K R O B A C K

posts.name: Chris Caruso
posts.cooked: I wish I would have tasted more wines my first 5 years into the hobby instead of just buying, buying. Now many of my oldest bottles are high scoring wines in styles I don’t enjoy.

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
No real regrets. My palate evolves and so do my preferences, and preparedness to pay depending on other committments at the time.
There are of course the “could’ve, would’ve” scenarios eg cases of 83 Grange at $300/case, but I always have something interesting to drink.

posts.name: Alan Eden
posts.cooked:
Every time we have a thread like this it always reminds me of music. When I was young I talked to people who had seen Elvis, Beatles, Zeppelin etc live, now I talk to young people and there like wow you saw U2 in a small theatre, you saw Pink Floyd, Bon Scott etc

Like everyone now in the 50’s & 60’s we remember DRC for $70, Lafite 82 for $70, cases of great wines like super seconds for $200. If you link this to my music story don’t you think there are going to people in 30 years saying wow 2013 Monte Bello for $100 and you didn’t fill you cellar !!! 2014 Montrose for $110 and you thought it was to high, were u crazy.

There are millions of wine choices and I don’t think you should have regrets, I know we all do myself included but think how lucky we are nowadays, great wine has never been so affordable

posts.name: Brian Buzzini
posts.cooked: Taking me 15years to GET to Burgundy…but I guess that’s par for the course. There’s Burgundy…than everything else…

posts.name: Randy Bowman
posts.cooked: Not checking the 16 case wine refrigerator daily after the earthquake last year to make sure it was “still” operating properly.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked:
If I could have known that premox was coming, which of course was impossible, I would have bought MUCH more pre-1995 White Burgundy so I would have plenty of well aged WB’s in my cellar to enjoy now and in the years to come…unfortunately my stock of such wines has dwindled. Sic Transit Gloria.

And I would have taken the money I spent on WB’s from a variety of producers in 1996, my favorite vintage when the wines were unaffected, and spent it all on 1996 Coche and Leflaive, whose wines of that vintage have never disappointed me. Unfortunately, in more recent vintages, the Coche I can no longer find or afford, and the Leflaives are no longer reliably non-poxed.

Oh, and I would have bought cases of Rousseau Chambertin in every vintage so I would not have to have to be so hesitant to open those precious few that I do have, since they are financially impossible for me to replenish.

Alan Eden:

Every time we have a thread like this it always reminds me of music. When I was young I talked to people who had seen Elvis, Beatles, Zeppelin etc live, now I talk to young people and there like wow you saw U2 in a small theatre, you saw Pink Floyd, Bon Scott etc

Like everyone now in the 50’s & 60’s we remember DRC for $70, Lafite 82 for $70, cases of great wines like super seconds for $200. If you link this to my music story don’t you think there are going to people in 30 years saying wow 2013 Monte Bello for $100 and you didn’t fill you cellar !!! 2014 Montrose for $110 and you thought it was to high, were u crazy.

There are millions of wine choices and I don’t think you should have regrets, I know we all do myself included but think how lucky we are nowadays, great wine has never been so affordable

Agreed. There are plenty of fantastic wines today which are affordable. Also plenty of fantastic wines which were affordable and are not anymore. Do we need to have regrets about those?
Why can’t we concentrate on the affordable ones and fill the cellar today with these? Otherwise it seems we all need many thousands of other people to like the same wines and drive the price high, before we get convinced that they are worthwhile. (I say this to myself first).

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Eric Ifune:

That mixed 1990 DRC case offered for $4500 keeps me awake at night.

Same for me . The offer was for 2 mixed cases at around $ 6500 each…

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
At the bottom of the market in 2008, I bought 2 immaculate bottles of 1985 Meo-Camuzet Clos Vougeot for what seemed like an impossibly high price to my still-in-law-school self, but impossible to turn down - maybe it was $125 or $150 each? They were immaculate in appearance, and both, when opened, were horrifically heat damaged.

What’s worse, I had made such a fuss about opening the bottle - saving the first for my 28th birthday dinner with my parents - and then the wine was undrinkably stewed and to the day my mother always asks me, when I’m bringing wine to dinner, not to bring an old burgundy, because old burgundy “tastes like old shoes like that one we drank at Aldea for your birthday that year”. Sigh.

posts.name: Dusty Gillson
posts.cooked: I regret only buying single bottles of everything I intended to cellar for the first 7 years of my wine obsession. I’d rather have half as many wines at this point with just as many bottles in my collection.

user_status.description: Waiting until it is socially acceptable to drink again.

I plead guilty, but ain’t it the truth.

PS - I saw Jim Morrison and The Doors in a small club in Greenwich Village and Pete Seeger playing the banjo off the back of a pickup truck and calling square dances when he was banned in the 1950s. I also have a bottle of 1975 Cheval Blanc with the original price sticker on it from when I bought it. $23.75.
posts.display_username: J a y H a c k

posts.name: Dave Butner
posts.cooked:
I should win this…

A couple of years ago at harvest, I was in the winery doing punchdowns on a fermenting bin of Syrah with about 30% whole cluster. Punchdowns are hard at the beginning of fermentation.

Some folks who were there tasting were observing, including a very stunningly beautiful and very intoxicated blonde woman. I said to her…“maybe you should jump in there and help with some foot stomping”. She said “really, you would let me?”

I said “sure, but you’ll have to take your pants off”, to which everyone laughed. She leaned over and whispered in my ear…“I’ll come back later when no one else is here, I’m not wearing any underwear”.

I told her I was just kidding around.

posts.name: harry spithoven
posts.cooked:
I sold 12 magnums of Lafite 1975.
The "experts"thought that it would be a shit wine forever.
Fortunatly I have som left and they ara GREAT now

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked:
I regret that I allowed myself to be so heavily influenced by what I thought I should be buying early on – what would be impressive at dinners and tastings, what I could tell people I bought – rather than what I most liked to drink. As a female collector, and a relatively young one 10+ years ago, I was often self-conscious about establishing my legitimacy. It meant I allocated a lot of resources to trying to be impressive. Not that I am not happy to have some of the wines I acquired in those days, but I do wish that I had been more secure in my tastes, and had gone long what pleased me most. Now I buy a lot more of the wines I reach for again and again rather than a few high-profile bottles.

And, of course, I wish I had started to like champagne sooner!

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Dave Butner:

I should win this…

A couple of years ago at harvest, I was in the winery doing punchdowns on a fermenting bin of Syrah with about 30% whole cluster. Punchdowns are hard at the beginning of fermentation.

Some folks who were there tasting were observing, including a very stunningly beautiful and very intoxicated blonde woman. I said to her…“maybe you should jump in there and help with some foot stomping”. She said “really, you would let me?”

I said “sure, but you’ll have to take your pants off”, to which everyone laughed. She leaned over and whispered in my ear…“I’ll come back later when no one else is here, I’m not wearing any underwear”.

I told her I was just kidding around.

I think there is nothing to “regret” here…at least you always have the story and the assumption that you made a mistake. Maybe she was just “kidding around” , but you’ll never know…and that’s good. The “road not taken” is always more full of memories…and fantasies.

I totally agree with this!

Even reading online blogs and sites such as this one, I still find myself wanting to get with “the cool kids” who seem to have an unending fount of old 1st crux burgundy, or the newest cult hipster bottles from hither and yon. And anytime I think I’m beyond all that, I just have to look at 10 bottles of Rhys Pinot that I can’t really stand, taking up space in my small cellar.

one of the nice things about this hobby, Sarah, is that there is always someone willing to buy your hand-me-downs, often for more than you paid. Sell them and buy your current favorites.

posts.name: Nathan Smyth
posts.cooked:
Exercising more self-control with the 2001 Haardter Mandelring Scheurebe Spatlese [Mulller-Catoir]. To include not being such a nice guy and giving away bottles as GIFTS. What a fool I am.

Second Place: Not cleaning out Bassin’s/MacArthur back when the 1996 Rene Engel Grands Echezeaux was still $129.99 [which was only a few short years ago - maybe even as recently as 2009/2010].

posts.name: Sarah Kirschbaum
posts.cooked: Thanks Berry. The sexism and condescension in the wine collecting world is deep, ingrained and insidious. Most of those who display it regularly have no idea they are guilty. But still my fault for allowing it to undermine my belief in my own taste. Fortunately, that I can fix, and largely have. The former…not so much.

posts.name: Kris_P@tten
posts.cooked:
alan weinberg:

if you could have a “do over” and take a vinous Mulligan, what would you do beside building the cellar twice as big?

For me, I would have bought a lot more “lesser” red Burgs in top vintages–AC and premier crus that would have allowed more quantity than the same dollars for grand crus.

I would have bought 2002 DRC mixed 12-PK for $6k…or $9k…or even $12k…

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: I wouldn’t have gifted so many of my bottles away about ten years ago. Man, I like being generous, but most of the recipients wouldn’t have appreciated the quality… Selfish, I know.
offered mixed cases of 99’, 00’, 01’ Truchot for an average of $55 per and passed… DOOOH!
oh and the 99’ DRC, DRC for $900 cost… I thought that was a ridiculous price…

Back in the early 90s I would buy a special bottle of Lopez de Heredia from Astor. They used to have locked in a see thru plastic covered case. I think the prices were in the $40s to $60 for 1968 and 1964. I would buy one for a special occasion and enjoy immediately. Always a pleasure. To see now what they go for makes me wish I bought cases. But back then I only had$$ for a bottle to enjoy immediately.

posts.name: Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow
posts.cooked:
Sarah Kirschbaum wrote:
I regret that I allowed myself to be so heavily influenced by what I thought I should be buying early on – what would be impressive at dinners and tastings, what I could tell people I bought – rather than what I most liked to drink. As a female collector, and a relatively young one 10+ years ago, I was often self-conscious about establishing my legitimacy. It meant I allocated a lot of resources to trying to be impressive. Not that I am not happy to have some of the wines I acquired in those days, but I do wish that I had been more secure in my tastes, and had gone long what pleased me most. Now I buy a lot more of the wines I reach for again and again rather than a few high-profile bottles.


Being “secure” in ones taste(and other traits) is crucial…and requires lots of self-awareness …and candor.

Being able to “reject” the outward pressures to like or be something is a good accomplishment…and constructive. And, for me, is one of life’s goals.

I’ve realized that modern haute cuisine, with its artfully contrived small plates and reimagination of items does little to nothing for me. I’ve only recently been able to say that to people when asked to join them at such places. After a meal at August in New Orleans last summer, I finally figured myself out in that regard.

Early recognition/“secure in ones tastes” is great. It leaves more time to be true to oneself. And, to ignore “establishing” much of anything via others’.

posts.name: Peter Moy
posts.cooked: About five years ago a friend and I were closing in on a deal to buy a magnificent cellar in Nimes. There were cases and cases of Bordeaux and Burgundy going back to the 1940s and 1950s, and some Jaboulet La Chapelle in good quantities from the 1960s and 1970s. I was all in, but at the last minute my friend got cold feet and pulled out, and the deal fell through.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked: Stuart & Sarah, very well said!

posts.name: Hank Gillespie
posts.cooked:
Funny, Stuart didn’t mention that he wished he had bought more 2004 Burgundy…but I guess he feels that he already has a large supply in his cellar. …just kidding, Stuart.

Actually I really can’t come up with any serious wine regrets, as I have very much enjoyed the places that “wine” has given me to seek out, and have continued to enjoy the wines I drink, and those I have drunk in the past. One of the things that I have especially enjoyed is the opportunity to meet and get to know the people I have encountered through wine.

Hank

posts.name: Ian Sutton
posts.cooked: We can all quote stories of wines that have rocketed in price that we didn’t buy, so mine is a Chardonnay (I suspect in hindsight from Macon region) with a light grey and light green pinstripe label that Oddbins were selling ~ 1991. About £6 a bottle and it was the first white wine to really wow me. The regret? I never noted the name of the wine/producer, so to this day have no idea who it was.

Stuart BeauneHead Niemtzow:

Sarah Kirschbaum wrote:
I regret that I allowed myself to be so heavily influenced by what I thought I should be buying early on – what would be impressive at dinners and tastings, what I could tell people I bought – rather than what I most liked to drink. As a female collector, and a relatively young one 10+ years ago, I was often self-conscious about establishing my legitimacy. It meant I allocated a lot of resources to trying to be impressive. Not that I am not happy to have some of the wines I acquired in those days, but I do wish that I had been more secure in my tastes, and had gone long what pleased me most. Now I buy a lot more of the wines I reach for again and again rather than a few high-profile bottles.


Being “secure” in ones taste(and other traits) is crucial…and requires lots of self-awareness …and candor.

Being able to “reject” the outward pressures to like or be something is a good accomplishment…and constructive. And, for me, is one of life’s goals.

I’ve realized that modern haute cuisine, with its artfully contrived small plates and reimagination of items does little to nothing for me. I’ve only recently been able to say that to people when asked to join them at such places. After a meal at August in New Orleans last summer, I finally figured myself out in that regard.

Early recognition/“secure in ones tastes” is great. It leaves more time to be true to oneself. And, to ignore “establishing” much of anything via others’.

In general I agree, but one has also to say that if one doesn’t question his/her own taste, one never learns to like tastes that at first may seem weird. I believe that at the beginning of the wine adventure all of us have had quite different tastes than after some years. Greatness in wine is not immediately evident and one needs to go through a learning phase to get there and appreciate it.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
P. Moy:

About five years ago a friend and I were closing in on a deal to buy a magnificent cellar in Nimes. There were cases and cases of Bordeaux and Burgundy going back to the 1940s and 1950s, and some Jaboulet La Chapelle in good quantities from the 1960s and 1970s. I was all in, but at the last minute my friend got cold feet and pulled out, and the deal fell through.

That reminds me, Peter, of a similar regret story. A close friend of mine was going through a divorce so wanted to sell his entire wine collection of about 1000 bottles of impressive old school bordeaux and california classics. I wanted to help my buddy out, knew that we would share many of the wines together anyway, but I had just closed on a new house and money was tight. He sold to another friend. I still dream about those '89 Haut Brions, '90 Latours, a few Petrus, and boatloads of Mondavi Reserve, Caymus from the early '90s, Dunn, Togni . . . . Pardon me while I go cry. He was selling them at his initial cost, plus three fridges.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Sarah Kirschbaum:

I regret that I allowed myself to be so heavily influenced by what I thought I should be buying early on – what would be impressive at dinners and tastings, what I could tell people I bought – rather than what I most liked to drink. As a female collector, and a relatively young one 10+ years ago, I was often self-conscious about establishing my legitimacy. It meant I allocated a lot of resources to trying to be impressive. Not that I am not happy to have some of the wines I acquired in those days, but I do wish that I had been more secure in my tastes, and had gone long what pleased me most. Now I buy a lot more of the wines I reach for again and again rather than a few high-profile bottles.

And, of course, I wish I had started to like champagne sooner!

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Sarah Kirschbaum:

Thanks Berry. The sexism and condescension in the wine collecting world is deep, ingrained and insidious. Most of those who display it regularly have no idea they are guilty. But still my fault for allowing it to undermine my belief in my own taste. Fortunately, that I can fix, and largely have. The former…not so much.

Adding to the condescension (although not to the sexism), I wish I had been more careful about some wines I opened for people who are not winos. I opened a few rare Burgundies for people who did not get them at all and regret doing so. There are a lot of excellent wines out there that have more fruit and are better to serve to people who haven’t developed the same love for wines that most of us have. I don’t mind the expense; what I mind is that I did not open these for friends that would have appreciated them.
